text.title: Test Architecture: Pyramid, Trophy, and Flaky Tests
text.summary: Test Pyramid (70% unit / 20% integration / 10% E2E) is the default strategy. Testing Trophy shifts weight to integration tests for UI-heavy products. Ice Cream Cone is the antipattern. Flaky tests must be quarantined, not tolerated.

section.title: Test Pyramid

rule.statement: Default to the 70/20/10 ratio: 70% unit tests, 20% integration tests, 10% E2E tests. Adjust the ratio based on architecture but never invert it.
rule.rationale: Unit tests at 50ms each vs E2E tests at 5 minutes each — 1,000 of each represents 50 seconds vs 83 hours of CI time. The pyramid keeps the test suite fast enough to run on every commit.

section.note: Testing Trophy (Kent C. Dodds) shifts the balance toward integration tests for React applications, where component integration errors dominate. Testing Honeycomb (Spotify) emphasizes contract tests for microservices. Choose based on where bugs actually originate.

antipattern.description: Inverted pyramid: many E2E tests, few unit tests, no integration tests.
antipattern.reason: E2E tests at 5 minutes each make CI prohibitively slow and provide poor diagnostic signal — a failure tells you something broke, not what or why. Unit tests at 50ms each provide fast, precise feedback and run before every commit.

rule.statement: Quarantine flaky tests immediately into a separate CI job; track failure rate and owner; set a re-integration SLA. Never let a flaky test block the main CI pipeline.
rule.rationale: A single flaky test in the main pipeline consumes ~40% of QA team attention (State of QA 2025) via reruns and false blame. Quarantine limits blast radius while root-cause investigation proceeds.

list.item: Use explicit waits (wait_for_selector, waitForResponse) — never sleep()
Use stable selectors (data-testid) — never XPath or text content
Mock all external services (HTTP, email, queue) — never call real endpoints in unit tests
Isolate test data — each test creates its own data, never reads shared mutable state
Run tests in parallel with process isolation — never share in-process state across workers
